docs.title: A fast, reliable digest for journalists and science communicators
docs.text:
Disinformation moves faster than most newsrooms can verify it. This project is a small experiment in closing that gap: a running collection of short, accessible briefings that translate recent open access scientific research into material journalists, editors, and science communicators can actually use — in a news story, a newsletter, or a social media post.

Each brief follows the same idea: take one peer-reviewed, open access paper on a topic currently in the public debate, and turn it into a short piece with a clear journalistic angle, a plain-language summary of what the research actually found, and a direct link back to the original source, so nothing here has to be taken on faith.

docs.title: What's in this collection
docs.text:
• Article 1 — Can chatbots be weaponized to mass-produce disinformation? A look at an ACL 2024 study that put ten major language models to the test.
  • Article 2 — When politicians talk about "disinformation," what do they actually mean? An analysis of a year of speeches in the Spanish Congress of Deputies.
  • Article 3 — How does misinformation on social media undermine vaccination campaigns and other public health efforts, and what can be done about it?
  • Article 4 — Fighting fire with fire: a global map of the organizations building AI tools to detect and counter disinformation.

docs.title: Why open access matters here
docs.text: Every brief in this collection is built on a study that is freely available to read in full, with no paywall. That is a deliberate choice: a project about improving public understanding of trustworthy information should itself be built on sources anyone can go back and check.

docs.title: A living, updatable format
docs.text: This site is built with MkDocs, so new briefs can be added quickly as new research and new debates emerge. on AI, climate, health, or any other topic where accurate, timely science communication makes a difference. Think of it less as a finished publication and more as a template: a repeatable workflow for turning credible research into content that is ready to publish, share, or adapt.

docs.title: Artificial Intelligence and Disinformation: How Dangerous Are Chatbots, Really?
Introduction
docs.text: Since ChatGPT and its competitors became part of everyday life, concern has grown that these tools could be used to mass-produce false content. This is no longer just a theoretical risk: a team of Slovak and Czech researchers put ten large language models (LLMs) to the test, asking each of them to write fake news articles based on twenty conspiracy narratives already circulating online, ranging from COVID-19 denialism to hoaxes about the US elections. The results, published in an open access paper, offer a useful snapshot for journalists and science communicators alike.

docs.title: The study: ten models put to the test
docs.text:
The study "Disinformation Capabilities of Large Language Models" (Vykopal et al., ACL 2024) evaluated models such as ChatGPT, GPT-4, GPT-3, Falcon, Vicuna, Llama-2, and Mistral, asking each one to generate three articles per narrative, for a total of 1,200 texts. Two human annotators then rated every text against six criteria: how well-formed the writing was, how closely it resembled a genuine news article, and, above all, whether the model agreed or disagreed with the disinformation it was asked to produce.

The most striking finding is that most models offered little resistance: they produced coherent, well-structured articles capable of introducing new supporting arguments for false narratives, often inventing names, events, and statistics that were never part of the original prompt. Only two models, Falcon and, to a lesser extent, ChatGPT, showed more caution, sometimes refusing to write the text or adding a disclaimer pointing out that the content was fictional. Vicuna and GPT-3 Davinci, on the other hand, proved the most willing to generate disinformation without hesitation, almost always aligning with the proposed narrative.

Another notable finding concerns the role of context in the prompt: when models were given a short abstract summarizing the narrative to support, the likelihood that the generated text aligned with the disinformation increased significantly. In other words, these systems turned out to be easily "steerable" toward biased content.

Finally, the researchers also tested whether automated tools could detect AI-generated text. The most effective detectors, based on the ELECTRA architecture, reached an F1 score of around 0.8 — an encouraging but not definitive result, still leaving room for error in real-world settings.

docs.title: Why this matters for journalism
docs.text:
For journalists and science communicators, the study offers concrete takeaways: generating disinformation with an LLM today costs only a few dollars and requires minimal skill, but some models also build in more effective safety mechanisms than others. Understanding which tools are relatively "safer" and which are more vulnerable to misuse can help newsrooms better assess the likely origin of suspicious content circulating online, and can inform the broader public debate on stricter rules for the development of these systems.

Source: Ivan Vykopal, Matúš Pikuliak, Ivan Srba, Robert Moro, Dominik Macko, Maria Bielikova, "Disinformation Capabilities of Large Language Models", Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2024), pp. 14830–14847. Available open access on ACL Anthology.

docs.title: AI and Disinformation
docs.text: Back to home

docs.title: Disinformation, Politics, and Rhetoric: What Spanish Lawmakers Really Talk About
Introduction
docs.text: "Fake president," "fake investiture," accusations of lying hurled back and forth across the chamber: in Spanish politics, the vocabulary of disinformation has become a routine weapon of parliamentary combat. But does this rhetoric reflect a genuine political commitment to fighting disinformation, or is it mostly a tool for scoring points against opponents? A study just published in Frontiers in Communication set out to answer exactly that question, analyzing over a thousand speeches given in Spain's Congress of Deputies during the first year of the COVID-19 pandemic — a period marked by high polarization and a documented surge in misinformation.

docs.title: The study: a year of parliamentary speeches under the microscope
docs.text:
The paper, "Disinformation in the Spanish public debate: an analysis of political speeches in the Congress of Deputies" (Domínguez-García, Velasco-Molpeceres and Pérez-Curiel, Frontiers in Communication, 2024), applied quantitative and qualitative content analysis to 1,115 parliamentary interventions delivered between January 2020 and January 2021, all containing at least one of six keywords: disinformation, hoax, lie, false, fake, and post-truth.

The first finding is almost counter-intuitive: despite the infodemic triggered by COVID-19, disinformation as a topic occupies a marginal place on the Spanish political agenda, appearing only about 17 times per plenary session on average. More tellingly, politicians rarely use the actual word "disinformation" (just 5.6% of the studied expressions) or "post-truth" (a mere 0.6%). Instead, they overwhelmingly prefer blunter terms like "lie" (45.2%) and "false" (32.6%) — words used less to analyze a systemic phenomenon and more to attack a specific opponent's specific claim.

This distinction matters. The researchers found that "disinformation" and "post-truth" tend to be used to describe broad, structural problems affecting society as a whole, while "lie," "hoax," "false," and "fake" are almost always pointed at a rival politician's individual statement. In practice, this means Spanish lawmakers rarely engage with disinformation as a policy issue to be solved — only 4.6% of the studied speeches proposed any concrete measure against it. The vast majority (84.9%) used these terms purely to attack the opposing side.

The analysis also uncovered a partisan pattern. Conservative opposition parties (PP and Vox) used disinformation-related vocabulary most frequently, while parties with a more nationalist profile, such as the Basque PNV and Catalan ERC, were comparatively more likely to use these terms to reinforce their own ideological narrative rather than simply to attack rivals. Government ministers, meanwhile, were the group most likely to use the term "disinformation" itself and to frame it constructively — though this happened in only a small fraction of cases.

docs.title: Why this matters for journalism
docs.text:
For journalists and science communicators covering political discourse, this study offers a useful diagnostic tool: it shows that when politicians invoke "disinformation," the word is more often a rhetorical weapon aimed at short-term polarization than a sign of genuine engagement with the problem. Recognizing this pattern can help reporters distinguish between substantive policy debate and purely confrontational rhetoric — and can inform coverage of how democratic institutions actually respond (or fail to respond) to one of the defining challenges of the information age.

Source: Ricardo Domínguez-García, Ana Velasco-Molpeceres, Concha Pérez-Curiel, "Disinformation in the Spanish public debate: an analysis of political speeches in the Congress of Deputies", Frontiers in Communication, 9:1363941 (2024). Open access under CC BY license, available at Frontiers in Communication.

docs.title: Disinformation and Political Communication
docs.text: Back to home

docs.title: Global Health Under Siege: How Social Media Misinformation Undermines Vaccination Campaigns
Introduction
docs.text: Vaccine hesitancy did not begin with COVID-19, but the pandemic gave it an unprecedented stage. As health authorities raced to roll out vaccines and communicate accurate guidance, social media platforms became fertile ground for exactly the opposite: rumors, conspiracy theories, and false claims that spread, according to research, faster than the truth itself. A 2022 study published in Health Education and Care takes stock of what we know about the relationship between social media misinformation and the success of public health interventions, and — more usefully for practitioners — what can actually be done about it.

docs.title: The study: connecting the dots between misinformation and vaccine hesitancy
docs.text:
The paper, "The impact of social media misinformation on public health interventions" (Aleksandric, Gaspar, Shaw Morawski and Mustata Wilson, Health Edu Care, 2022), is a narrative review that pulls together evidence from dozens of prior studies to map how misinformation on platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and YouTube interferes with vaccination and other public health efforts. The authors adopt a broad working definition: misinformation is "false or inaccurate information that is deliberately created and intentionally or unintentionally propagated" — deliberately blurring the line with disinformation, since most readers cannot tell the two apart anyway.

One of the review's central findings echoes a now-famous 2018 study: false claims spread faster across social networks than accurate information does. When people repeatedly encounter posts exaggerating vaccine risks, many respond by refusing vaccination altogether — a dynamic the authors link directly to the World Health Organization's three classic drivers of vaccine hesitancy: confidence (mistrust in the health system), complacency (underestimating disease risk), and convenience (barriers to access). The review traces hesitancy well beyond COVID-19, citing cases of flu vaccine refusal in the UK, Canada, and Saudi Arabia, and polio vaccine rejection in Pakistan and Nigeria, often fueled by fears about infertility or foreign conspiracies.

A particularly useful thread in the paper connects misinformation to social vulnerability: US states with a higher Social Vulnerability Index — meaning communities less equipped to cope with a crisis like a disease outbreak — tend to show both lower health literacy and lower vaccination rates, alongside more negative sentiment on social media. In other words, misinformation does not spread evenly; it hits hardest exactly where health systems are already under strain, including among ethnic minority and disadvantaged groups.

Rather than treating social media purely as the villain, the authors also highlight its potential as a countermeasure. They suggest concrete steps health organizations can take: posting clear, factual content about interventions; enlisting trusted, influential voices to amplify accurate messages; creating dedicated public pages where people can ask health professionals direct questions; and using social media data itself to identify which populations are most exposed to false claims, so outreach can be targeted more precisely.

Finally, the review is candid about the obstacles researchers face in this space: limited access to social media data (most platforms only release a partial, public sample of posts), a shortage of interdisciplinary expertise combining public health and data science, insufficient funding, and the fact that most studies are confined to a single language, limiting their global relevance.

docs.title: Why this matters for journalism
docs.text:
For journalists and science communicators, this review offers a map of where disinformation does the most damage — and a reminder that combating it is not just a content-moderation problem but a resourcing one: without better data access, more interdisciplinary research, and sustained funding, public health communicators will keep fighting misinformation with one hand tied behind their back. Framing stories around these structural gaps, rather than treating each viral hoax as an isolated event, can help audiences understand why misinformation keeps recurring across different health topics and different countries.

Source: Ana Aleksandric, Michael Gaspar, Toria Shaw Morawski, Gabriela Mustata Wilson, "The impact of social media misinformation on public health interventions", Health Education and Care, 7:1-6 (2022). Open access under the Creative Commons Attribution License, available at Health Education and Care.

docs.title: Global Health and Disinformation
docs.text: Back to home

docs.title: Fighting Fire with Fire: Mapping How AI Is Being Used Against Disinformation
Introduction
docs.text: If artificial intelligence can be used to mass-produce disinformation, can it also be turned against it? Governments, tech companies, and civil society have bet heavily that it can, funding dozens of AI-powered fact-checking tools, detection systems, and verification platforms over the past several years. But with so many initiatives launched independently across different countries, a basic question has gone largely unanswered: who is actually doing what, and how do these efforts relate to one another? A new study published in Frontiers in Political Science takes on exactly that question, mapping the global landscape of AI-driven counter-disinformation projects for the first time.

docs.title: The study: tracing the web of counter-disinformation initiatives
docs.text:
The paper, "The use of artificial intelligence in counter-disinformation: a world wide (web) mapping" (Pilati and Venturini, Frontiers in Political Science, 2025), does not analyze how well these AI tools work — instead, it maps the network of organizations building them, using an approach called hyperlink network analysis. The idea is simple but powerful: websites tend to link to others that share their goals or worldview, so studying who links to whom reveals the hidden communities behind a field. The researchers identified 81 active websites of organizations using or developing AI against disinformation, then crawled and mapped nearly 400 hyperlinks connecting them.

The resulting map reveals three distinct clusters. The largest, dominated by European initiatives, is built around EU-funded Horizon research projects and public research institutes, often working hand-in-hand with public IT infrastructure providers. A second, smaller cluster sits in the United States and is centered on university research (institutions like MIT and Harvard feature prominently) and think tanks, largely funded by private tech companies rather than public grants. The third cluster consists of established fact-checking organizations — familiar names like Snopes, PolitiFact, and FullFact — many of which have received direct funding from Google or partnered with Meta's fact-checking program.

Beyond geography, the study identifies a meaningful difference in strategy. European and American research initiatives tend to work "upstream": they build AI tools meant to improve the overall quality of the information environment before disinformation even spreads widely — for example, systems that help verify authentic content or detect deepfakes. Fact-checking organizations, by contrast, generally work "downstream," using AI to speed up the process of debunking claims after they have already gone viral. Despite this difference, the authors note one constant across all approaches: human oversight remains indispensable — none of the mapped initiatives treat AI as a fully autonomous solution.

The study also flags real limitations in the current ecosystem. Almost the entire mapped network is concentrated in Europe and North America, with only isolated exceptions — such as Argentina's Chequeado — representing the Global South. Civil society groups beyond fact-checkers, such as media watchdogs and human rights organizations, are also strikingly underrepresented, suggesting that many of these organizations have not yet built AI capacity into their work. Interestingly, the researchers also found that many now-defunct projects were American initiatives launched during the peak of "fake news" panic around 2016–2017, which had aimed for fully automated content moderation — an ambition that ran into ethical and practical limits.

docs.title: Why this matters for journalism
docs.text:
For journalists and communicators, this mapping offers a practical orientation to a fragmented field: it clarifies which organizations are building the tools reporters might rely on for verification, who is funding them, and where the gaps are — particularly the near-total absence of AI counter-disinformation capacity outside Europe and North America. Understanding these dynamics can help newsrooms choose which tools to trust, and can inform advocacy for expanding this kind of infrastructure to regions currently left out of the conversation.

Source: Federico Pilati, Tommaso Venturini, "The use of artificial intelligence in counter-disinformation: a world wide (web) mapping", Frontiers in Political Science, 7:1517726 (2025). Open access under the Creative Commons Attribution License, available at Frontiers in Political Science.

docs.title: AI and Counter-Disinformation
